To find the largest possible number of interior angles greater than $180^\circ$ in an $n$-gon, we need to consider the properties of the interior angles of a polygon.

The sum of the interior angles of an $n$-gon is given by the formula $(n-2) \cdot 180^\circ$. Since all sides of the $n$-gon have the same length, the $n$-gon is a regular polygon.

For an interior angle to be greater than $180^\circ$, the adjacent angles must be less than $180^\circ$. In a regular polygon, the interior angles are equal, so if one angle is greater than $180^\circ$, the others must be less than $180^\circ$.

The largest possible number of interior angles greater than $180^\circ$ occurs when the $n$-gon is a triangle, which has 3 angles. However, in a triangle, all angles are less than $180^\circ$, so this is not possible.
